title: Orange zest cold-process soap
subtitle: A bright citrus bar with dried orange zest folded in at trace, on an olive, coconut, and sweet almond base
excerpt: Dried orange zest gives this cold-process bar a speckled amber appearance and a light textural interest. Sweet orange essential oil is added at trace for fragrance. The oil base of olive, coconut, and sweet almond produces a lathering, conditioning bar. Orange zest fades in colour during the cure period, settling to amber flecks against a cream bar.
sourceNotes: SAP values: olive oil 0.134, coconut oil 0.190, sweet almond oil 0.136. 5% superfat; 33% lye discount. Reference: Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997). Sweet orange essential oil (Citrus sinensis) accelerates trace in high concentrations; the dosage here is conservative. Dried orange peel as a soap additive: Bramble Berry natural colourants and additives guide (2023). Orange EO in cold-process soap: the d-limonene content can cause acceleration; test at low trace.

recipe.batchNotes: Recalculate lye weight for any oil changes. The zest and essential oil are additives; they do not affect the lye calculation.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Allow 2 days in the mould before unmoulding. Minimum 4-week cure; 6 weeks for a harder bar.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Fully cured bars keep two years in a cool, dark place on an open rack. Discard any bar with orange oily spots (DOS) or a sharp paint-like smell.

glossaryTerms.definition: The point at which the soap batter has emulsified and thickened to a custard-like consistency. The moment to stir in zest and essential oils.
The percentage of oil left free and unsaponified in the finished bar. At 5%, the bar is mild and conditioning.
The reaction between fat and lye that produces soap and glycerine.

projectSchedule.title: Unmould and cut
projectSchedule.body: Unmould after 2 days. Cut into bars wearing protective gloves.

projectSchedule.title: Four-week check
projectSchedule.body: Test a sliver on skin. The zest flecks will have settled to amber. Ready to use.

projectSchedule.title: Cure complete
projectSchedule.body: Fully cured. Hard, mild, and stable for two years.

content.text: Dried orange zest stirred into the soap batter at

gives this bar amber flecks and a light textural surface. Sweet orange essential oil adds a fresh citrus scent. The oil base of 55% olive, 30% coconut, and 15% sweet almond makes a conditioning bar with good lather. At 5%
and 33% lye discount, it is mild for daily use. Note: sweet orange essential oil contains d-limonene, which can accelerate
speed and cause rapid thickening. Work at light trace and pour quickly.
Put on protective gloves and goggles before starting.

items.prepNote: 30% of oil weight; melt if solid
Finely dried and ground; added at trace
Added at trace; work quickly as orange EO can accelerate

content.text: In a ventilated space with goggles and protective gloves on, weigh the lye separately. Pour distilled water into the HDPE pitcher. Add lye to water in a slow, steady stream. Never reverse. The solution heats to around 80°C; stand back. Cool to 38 to 42°C.
Combine and blend to light trace

Combine all oils in a heat-safe bowl; melt the coconut oil if solid. Cool to 38 to 42°C. Pour the lye solution into the oils. Stick-blend in short bursts to reach a very light trace. The batter should look like thin custard. Stir in the orange zest and sweet orange essential oil quickly and blend for 5 seconds. Pour immediately into the mould.
Wrap the mould in a towel. Leave for 2 days. Unmould and cut into bars wearing protective gloves. Cure on an open rack for 4 to 6 weeks in a cool, dry room, turning every two weeks.
Two years on an open rack in a cool, dark place. Discard on signs of DOS (orange oily spots) or a paint-like smell.
